name: Iran
tagline: From Cyrus the Great's first world empire to the Islamic Republic.
summary: Iran is heir to one of the world's oldest civilisations. On the south-western plateau the kingdom of Elam flourished from the early third millennium BCE, but it was Cyrus the Great's founding of the Achaemenid (Persian) Empire around 550 BCE that created history's first true world empire, stretching from the Aegean to the Indus. Persia outlasted conquest by Alexander, was reborn under the Parthians and Sasanians, and after the Arab conquest of the seventh century became a heartland of the Islamic golden age. The Safavids made Iran a Twelver Shia state in 1501; the Qajar and Pahlavi shahs wrestled with modernity and foreign power; and in 1979 a revolution swept away the monarchy and established the Islamic Republic of Iran under Ayatollah Khomeini.

founding.label: Founding of the Achaemenid (Persian) Empire by Cyrus the Great
founding.detail: Around 550 BCE Cyrus the Great (Cyrus II) overthrew the Median king Astyages and forged the Achaemenid Empire, the first Persian empire and the largest the world had yet seen. After capturing Babylon in 539 BCE he issued the famous Cyrus Cylinder, often cited as an early charter of tolerance. Persia's long imperial story passed through Hellenistic, Parthian, Sasanian, Islamic, Turkic, Mongol and Safavid phases before the Pahlavi monarchy fell to the Islamic Revolution of 1979, which on 1 April 1979 established the Islamic Republic of Iran.

quickFacts.label: Imperial founding
quickFacts.value: Achaemenid Empire under Cyrus the Great, c. 550 BCE

quickFacts.label: First ceremonial capital
quickFacts.value: Pasargadae; later Persepolis, founded by Darius I c. 518 BCE

quickFacts.label: State religion (1501)
quickFacts.value: Twelver Shia Islam, established by the Safavids

quickFacts.label: Earlier civilisation
quickFacts.value: Elam, centred on Susa, from c. 3200 BCE

eras.title: Elam and the Achaemenid Empire
eras.standfirst: From the ancient kingdom of Elam to Cyrus the Great's first world empire, the Iranian plateau gave rise to one of antiquity's most powerful and tolerant states.
body: Long before the Persians, the kingdom of Elam flourished in the south-west of present-day Iran, centred on the city of Susa, from around 3200 BCE. Closely entwined with neighbouring Mesopotamia, Elam reached its height in the second millennium BCE before being ground down between Babylon and Assyria, with Susa sacked by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal around 647 BCE. Meanwhile Indo-Iranian peoples — the Medes and the Persians — settled the plateau, and the Medes built a kingdom centred on Ecbatana.

Around 550 BCE Cyrus II, a Persian vassal, rose against and defeated his Median overlord Astyages, uniting Medes and Persians under the Achaemenid house. Within two decades he had absorbed Lydia and, in 539 BCE, the great city of Babylon. The Cyrus Cylinder, composed after that conquest, records his restoration of temples and the return of deported peoples, and is often celebrated as an early statement of religious tolerance. By the reign of Darius I (522–486 BCE) the empire stretched from the Aegean and Egypt to the Indus.

Darius organised this vast realm into provinces called satrapies, built the Royal Road from Susa to Sardis, standardised coinage, and began the ceremonial capital of Persepolis around 518 BCE. Persian kings practised a broadly Zoroastrian faith and tolerated the many religions of their subjects. The empire's expansion into the Aegean brought it into collision with the Greek city-states in the Greco-Persian Wars of the early fifth century BCE.

Persian power endured for two centuries until Alexander the Great of Macedon shattered the Achaemenid army at Gaugamela in 331 BCE. The following year Alexander entered and burned Persepolis, and the last Achaemenid king, Darius III, was killed in flight, ending the dynasty in 330 BCE.

events.title: The kingdom of Elam and the city of Susa
events.summary: Elam, one of the earliest civilisations of the region, flourishes around its capital Susa in south-western Iran, trading and warring with Mesopotamia until Susa is sacked by the Assyrians around 647 BCE.

events.title: Cyrus the Great founds the Achaemenid Empire
events.summary: Cyrus II overthrows the Median king Astyages and unites the Medes and Persians, founding the Achaemenid (Persian) Empire — the first Persian world empire.

events.title: Conquest of Babylon and the Cyrus Cylinder
events.summary: Cyrus captures Babylon and issues the Cyrus Cylinder, a Babylonian cuneiform document recording his restoration of temples and return of deported peoples, often hailed as an early charter of tolerance.

events.title: Darius I builds Persepolis
events.summary: Darius the Great organises the empire into satrapies, builds the Royal Road and standardised coinage, and begins the magnificent ceremonial capital of Persepolis, today a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: Alexander conquers Persia and burns Persepolis
events.summary: After defeating Darius III at Gaugamela (331 BCE), Alexander the Great enters and burns Persepolis and the last Achaemenid king is killed, ending the empire in 330 BCE.

figures.name: Cyrus the Great (Cyrus II)
figures.blurb: Founder of the first Persian Empire, who united the Medes and Persians and built a realm stretching from Anatolia to Central Asia. Remembered for the comparatively tolerant administration recorded on the Cyrus Cylinder and revered in Iran as the father of the nation.

figures.name: Darius I (Darius the Great)
figures.blurb: Consolidator of the empire who created the satrapy system, the Royal Road and a standard coinage, and built Persepolis. Under him the Achaemenid realm reached its greatest extent, from the Aegean to the Indus valley.

pullquote.text: I am Cyrus, king of the world, great king, mighty king, king of Babylon.

eras.title: Hellenistic, Parthian and Sasanian Persia
eras.standfirst: After Alexander, Persia revived under the Parthians and reached new heights of culture and statecraft under the Sasanians, the last great pre-Islamic empire of Iran.
body: Alexander's death in 323 BCE left his empire to his generals, and Iran fell to the Seleucids, a Hellenistic dynasty that fused Greek and Persian culture. Greek rule on the plateau was never secure, and from the mid-third century BCE the Parni, a nomadic people, established the Parthian (Arsacid) Empire under Arsaces I, conventionally dated to 247 BCE. The Parthians revived Persian traditions, ruled from Ctesiphon, and for nearly five centuries proved Rome's most formidable rival in the East.

In 224 CE Ardashir I, a ruler from Persis, overthrew the last Parthian king Artabanus IV and founded the Sasanian Empire. The Sasanians cast themselves as restorers of Achaemenid glory, built a centralised state, and made Zoroastrianism the official religion, with a powerful priesthood and fire temples across the realm. Sasanian art, architecture and administration deeply influenced both the later Islamic world and Byzantium.

For four centuries the Sasanians and the Romans, later the Byzantines, fought along their shared frontier. The exhausting wars of 602–628 CE drained both empires. Weakened and divided, the Sasanian state proved unable to withstand the Arab Muslim armies that swept out of Arabia. After decisive defeats, above all at Nahavand in 642 CE, the empire collapsed, and the last shah, Yazdegerd III, was killed in 651 CE.

events.title: The Parthian (Arsacid) Empire
events.summary: Arsaces I founds the Parthian Empire in north-eastern Iran, reviving Persian traditions and becoming Rome's chief rival in the East for nearly five centuries.

events.title: Ardashir I founds the Sasanian Empire
events.summary: Ardashir I overthrows the last Parthian king and establishes the Sasanian Empire, the last great pre-Islamic Persian state, with its capital at Ctesiphon.

events.title: Zoroastrianism as state religion
events.summary: The Sasanian kings establish Zoroastrianism, the faith attributed to the prophet Zoroaster, as the official religion of Iran, supported by a powerful priesthood and network of fire temples.

events.title: Exhausting wars with Byzantium
events.summary: The last great Byzantine–Sasanian war drains both empires militarily and economically, leaving Persia dangerously weakened on the eve of the Arab conquest.

events.title: Battle of Nahavand — the 'Victory of Victories'
events.summary: Arab Muslim forces crush the Sasanian army at Nahavand, breaking organised Persian resistance; the empire finally collapses with the death of Yazdegerd III in 651 CE.

figures.name: Ardashir I
figures.blurb: A ruler of Persis who defeated the last Parthian king and founded the Sasanian dynasty, casting it as the rightful heir of the Achaemenids. He centralised the state and elevated Zoroastrianism as its official religion.

eras.title: The Arab Conquest and Islamic Persia — the Golden Age
eras.standfirst: Conquered by the Arabs, Persia gradually embraced Islam yet preserved its language and identity, becoming a powerhouse of science, philosophy and poetry in the Islamic golden age.
body: The fall of the Sasanians brought Iran into the world of Islam. Over the following centuries most Iranians converted, and Persia was governed first by the Umayyad and then the Abbasid caliphs. Yet Persia was no passive province: Persian administrators, scholars and the New Persian language, written in Arabic script, became central to Islamic civilisation. The Abbasid Revolution of 750 CE, which drew heavily on support from Khorasan, shifted the caliphate's centre of gravity eastward to a new capital at Baghdad, built near the old Sasanian heartland.

From the eighth to the thirteenth centuries Persia stood at the heart of the Islamic golden age. In Baghdad's House of Wisdom, founded under the Abbasids, Persian, Arab and other scholars translated and extended the learning of Greece, India and Persia. Iranian thinkers made landmark contributions: the physician-philosopher Avicenna (Ibn Sina) wrote the Canon of Medicine, used in Europe for centuries; al-Khwarizmi pioneered algebra; and the polymath Omar Khayyam reformed the calendar and wrote celebrated quatrains.

As caliphal authority weakened, Persian-speaking dynasties such as the Samanids of Bukhara fostered a renaissance of Persian culture. Around 1010 CE the poet Ferdowsi completed the Shahnameh, the 'Book of Kings', a vast epic that preserved Iran's pre-Islamic legends and helped secure the survival of the Persian language. This flowering of literature, science and art unfolded even as new Turkic powers, above all the Seljuks, rose to dominate the region.

events.title: Islamisation of Persia begins
events.summary: With the Sasanian state destroyed, Iran is incorporated into the Islamic caliphate; over the following centuries most of its people convert to Islam while retaining the Persian language and much of their culture.

events.title: Abbasid Revolution and the rise of Baghdad
events.summary: Backed by support from Persian Khorasan, the Abbasids overthrow the Umayyads and found Baghdad, drawing heavily on Persian administrative and intellectual traditions.

events.title: Persia at the heart of the Islamic golden age
events.summary: In the House of Wisdom and beyond, Persian scholars such as Avicenna, al-Khwarizmi and Omar Khayyam advance medicine, mathematics and astronomy during the Islamic golden age.

events.title: Ferdowsi completes the Shahnameh
events.summary: The poet Ferdowsi finishes the Shahnameh, the 'Book of Kings', an epic of some 50,000 couplets that preserves Iran's mythic and pre-Islamic history and anchors the Persian language.

figures.name: Avicenna (Ibn Sina)
figures.blurb: One of the foremost thinkers of the Islamic golden age, whose Canon of Medicine remained a standard medical text in Europe and the Islamic world for centuries. His philosophical works profoundly shaped both later Islamic and European thought.

figures.name: Ferdowsi
figures.blurb: Author of the Shahnameh, the national epic of Iran, whose verse preserved the heroic legends of pre-Islamic Persia and helped ensure the survival and prestige of the Persian language.

pullquote.text: Much have I laboured, much I read, of ancient lore and tales of yore: this Book of Kings shall be my deed.

eras.title: Turkic and Mongol Rule and the Safavids
eras.standfirst: After devastating Mongol invasions, Iran was reborn under the Safavids, who in 1501 made Twelver Shia Islam the state religion and forged the modern Iranian nation.
body: From 1219 the armies of Genghis Khan poured into Iran, sacking great cities and inflicting catastrophic loss of life. His grandson Hulagu Khan completed the conquest, seizing the Ismaili fortress of Alamut in 1256 and destroying Abbasid Baghdad in 1258. The Mongols established the Ilkhanate (c. 1260–1335), which, though founded in bloodshed, eventually adopted Islam and patronised Persian art and historiography. In the late fourteenth century the conqueror Timur (Tamerlane) again ravaged and then ruled the region, and the Timurid courts became renowned centres of Persianate culture.

Out of this turbulence rose the Safavids. In 1501 the young Ismail I captured Tabriz, proclaimed himself shah, and declared Twelver Shia Islam the official religion of his realm. This decisive act set Iran apart from its Sunni Ottoman and Uzbek neighbours and forged a religious identity that endures to this day, often at the cost of conflict and forced conversion.

The Safavid state reached its zenith under Shah Abbas I, the Great (reigned 1588–1629), who reformed the army, curbed the power of the Turkic tribal aristocracy, and in 1598 moved his capital to Isfahan. There he created the magnificent Naqsh-e Jahan (Imam) Square, framed by mosques, palaces and a grand bazaar — a masterpiece of Safavid urban design now a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Isfahan became one of the world's great cities, but after Abbas the dynasty declined, and in 1722 Afghan invaders sacked the capital, with the dynasty formally ending in 1736.

events.title: Mongol invasion of Iran
events.summary: Genghis Khan's armies invade Iran, devastating its cities; his grandson Hulagu completes the conquest, taking Alamut in 1256 and destroying Baghdad in 1258, and founds the Ilkhanate.

events.title: Timur and the Timurid era
events.summary: Timur (Tamerlane) conquers the lands of the former Ilkhanate, founding the Timurid empire whose courts become celebrated centres of Persianate art and learning.

events.title: Ismail I founds the Safavid dynasty and Shia Iran
events.summary: Ismail I captures Tabriz, proclaims himself shah, and declares Twelver Shia Islam the state religion, setting Iran apart from its Sunni neighbours and shaping its identity to the present day.

events.title: Shah Abbas moves the capital to Isfahan
events.summary: At the height of Safavid power, Shah Abbas the Great relocates the capital to Isfahan and builds the grand Naqsh-e Jahan (Imam) Square, a UNESCO World Heritage masterpiece of urban design.

events.title: Afghan sack of Isfahan and Safavid decline
events.summary: Afghan invaders besiege and sack Isfahan, shattering Safavid power; the dynasty formally ends in 1736, opening a period of instability before the rise of the Qajars.

figures.name: Ismail I
figures.blurb: Founder of the Safavid state who at fourteen seized Tabriz and proclaimed Twelver Shia Islam the religion of Iran, an act that decisively shaped the country's religious and national identity.

figures.name: Shah Abbas I (the Great)
figures.blurb: The greatest Safavid ruler, who reorganised the army and administration, drove back the Ottomans and Uzbeks, and made Isfahan a dazzling imperial capital celebrated as 'half the world'.

eras.title: The Qajars, Constitutional Revolution and the Pahlavi Shahs
eras.standfirst: Under the Qajars Iran lost ground to Russia and Britain, won a pioneering constitution in 1906, and then under the Pahlavi shahs pursued forced modernisation that ended in revolution.
body: From the late eighteenth century the Qajar dynasty ruled Iran, but its long nineteenth century was marked by weakness before the European powers. Wars with Russia cost Iran the Caucasus, and Britain and Russia carved out spheres of influence, securing lucrative concessions over tobacco, oil and finance. Resentment at this foreign penetration and at royal misrule fuelled demands for reform.

In 1905–1906 a broad coalition of merchants, clerics and intellectuals forced Mozaffar al-Din Shah to grant a constitution and a national parliament, the Majles. This Constitutional Revolution, the first of its kind in the Middle East, was a landmark in Iranian political history, though the constitution was soon suspended and the movement crushed with Russian help by 1911. The discovery of oil in 1908 made Iran a strategic prize and bound its economy ever more tightly to foreign companies.

In 1921 the army officer Reza Khan seized power in a coup, and in 1925 the Majles deposed the last Qajar shah and made him Reza Shah Pahlavi. He drove a programme of authoritarian modernisation — building railways, schools, a centralised state and a secular legal system — and in 1935 had the country known internationally as Iran. After Allied forces deposed him in 1941, his son Mohammad Reza Pahlavi took the throne.

In 1951 the popular prime minister Mohammad Mosaddegh nationalised the British-controlled oil industry, but in August 1953 he was overthrown in a coup organised by the American CIA and British intelligence. The restored shah ruled increasingly autocratically, pursuing rapid Westernising reform while suppressing dissent, until mounting opposition exploded into the revolution of 1978–79.

events.title: Persian Constitutional Revolution
events.summary: After mass protests, Mozaffar al-Din Shah grants a constitution and the first Majles (parliament), a pioneering experiment in constitutional government in the Middle East.

events.title: Discovery of oil
events.summary: Oil is struck in south-western Iran, making the country a strategic prize and tying its economy to foreign-controlled concessions for decades to come.

events.title: Reza Khan founds the Pahlavi dynasty
events.summary: Having seized power in a 1921 coup, Reza Khan deposes the last Qajar shah and is crowned Reza Shah Pahlavi, launching an authoritarian drive to modernise and centralise Iran.

events.title: The CIA-backed coup against Mosaddegh
events.summary: Prime Minister Mohammad Mosaddegh, who had nationalised the oil industry, is overthrown in August 1953 in a coup organised by the CIA and British intelligence, restoring the shah's power.

figures.name: Reza Shah Pahlavi
figures.blurb: A Cossack officer who rose through a 1921 coup to found the Pahlavi monarchy. He modernised Iran's infrastructure, army and law along secular, centralising lines, and in 1935 had the country called Iran internationally.

figures.name: Mohammad Mosaddegh
figures.blurb: A nationalist statesman who nationalised the British-controlled oil industry in 1951, becoming a symbol of Iranian sovereignty. His overthrow in the 1953 coup deepened resentment of foreign interference that fed the 1979 revolution.

eras.title: The Islamic Republic
eras.standfirst: The 1979 revolution swept away the monarchy and created the Islamic Republic of Iran, a theocratic state that has reshaped the country and the wider Middle East.
body: In 1978 mass protests, strikes and demonstrations against the shah's autocracy, inequality and Westernisation convulsed Iran. On 16 January 1979 Mohammad Reza Pahlavi fled the country, and on 1 February the exiled cleric Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini returned in triumph to Tehran. The monarchy collapsed on 11 February 1979. In a referendum held on 30–31 March, an overwhelming majority voted to replace it with an Islamic republic, which Khomeini proclaimed on 1 April 1979.

A new constitution, approved later that year, vested ultimate authority in a Supreme Leader and the doctrine of velayat-e faqih, the 'guardianship of the jurist', alongside an elected president and parliament. The seizure of the United States embassy in Tehran in November 1979, and the 444-day hostage crisis that followed, set the new republic on a collision course with the West that has endured for decades.

Barely a year after the revolution, Iraq invaded in September 1980, beginning a grinding eight-year war that cost hundreds of thousands of lives before Iran accepted a UN-brokered ceasefire under Resolution 598 in August 1988. In the decades since, the Islamic Republic has navigated reform and retrenchment at home, regional rivalries and proxy conflicts abroad, and a long confrontation with Western powers over its nuclear programme and sanctions, while periodic waves of popular protest have tested the system.

events.title: The Islamic Revolution
events.summary: The shah flees in January 1979 and Ayatollah Khomeini returns from exile; the monarchy collapses on 11 February, ending some 2,500 years of Iranian kingship.

events.title: Proclamation of the Islamic Republic
events.summary: After a referendum on 30–31 March approves the change by an overwhelming majority, Khomeini proclaims the Islamic Republic of Iran on 1 April 1979.

events.title: US embassy seized and hostage crisis
events.summary: Militant students storm the US embassy in Tehran, holding 52 Americans for 444 days and setting the new republic on a long course of confrontation with the West.

events.title: The Iran–Iraq War
events.summary: Iraq invades in September 1980, igniting a devastating eight-year war; Iran accepts UN Security Council Resolution 598 and a ceasefire takes effect on 20 August 1988.

figures.name: Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini
figures.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic
figures.blurb: A Shia cleric whose opposition to the shah from exile made him the figurehead of the 1979 revolution. He returned to lead the new Islamic Republic, enshrining clerical rule through the doctrine of velayat-e faqih.

pullquote.text: This is the day of God's victory and the victory of the nation.
pullquote.attribution: Ayatollah Khomeini, on proclaiming the Islamic Republic, 1 April 1979

sources.label: Alexander the Great & the Burning of Persepolis
Ancient Iran — The Hellenistic and Parthian periods
The Phases, History, and Legacy of the Arab Conquests (632–750 CE)
Meidan Emam, Esfahan — UNESCO World Heritage List
